title: Modern Fermentation Equipment Guide
subtitle: From mason jars to ceramic crocks — everything you need to start fermenting at home

sections.content: One of the great things about fermentation is that you can start with equipment you already own. A glass jar, some salt, and fresh vegetables are genuinely all you need [cite:c1]. That said, purpose-built fermentation equipment can make the process more convenient, more consistent, and more enjoyable — especially as you scale up or tackle more ambitious projects. This guide covers everything from the bare essentials to advanced setups, helping you decide what's worth investing in at each stage of your fermentation journey [[article:fermentation-tools-through-history]].
You can begin fermenting today with items from your kitchen. Here is the absolute minimum you need for a basic vegetable ferment like [[recipe:sauerkraut-classic]]:

items: **A glass jar** (mason jar, bail-top jar, or any wide-mouth glass container) — 1 litre / 1 quart is a good starting size
**Non-iodized salt** — sea salt, kosher salt, or pickling salt [[article:salt-and-fermentation]]
**A knife and cutting board** — for preparing vegetables
**A kitchen scale** (strongly recommended) — for accurate salt measurements by weight
**Something to keep vegetables submerged** — a smaller jar filled with water, a zip-lock bag of brine, or even a clean rock

sections.title: Don't Let Equipment Be a Barrier
sections.content: Humans have been fermenting for over 10,000 years without any specialized equipment. A glass jar and salt are enough to make sauerkraut, pickles, kimchi, and dozens of other ferments. Buy specialized gear only when you know you enjoy the hobby and want more convenience.

The vessel you ferment in affects convenience, airflow management, and batch size. Glass is the most popular material for home fermenters because it is non-reactive, transparent (so you can monitor progress), easy to clean, and widely available [cite:c2]. Ceramic crocks are traditional and excellent for larger batches. Avoid metal containers (the acid will corrode them) and most plastics (which can leach chemicals and absorb odors).

items: **Mason jars (Ball, Kerr)** — Inexpensive, widely available, and work perfectly for small batches. Wide-mouth versions are easier to pack and clean.
**Bail-top (Fido-style) jars** — The rubber gasket allows CO₂ to escape while limiting oxygen ingress. A simple and effective semi-airlock system.

**Ceramic fermentation crocks** — Traditional vessels with water-seal gutters that act as built-in airlocks. Ideal for large batches (5–10 litres). Heavier and more expensive, but beautiful and functional.
**Food-grade plastic buckets** — Useful for very large batches. Ensure they are food-grade HDPE (recycling code 2). Not ideal for long-term storage due to oxygen permeability.

sections.content: Airlocks allow carbon dioxide produced during fermentation to escape while preventing oxygen from entering the jar. They are not strictly necessary — people fermented successfully without them for millennia — but they reduce surface mold and kahm yeast and eliminate the need to "burp" jars manually [cite:c1].

items: **Silicone airlock lids** — Fit directly onto mason jars. Simple one-way valves that require no water. Very popular with beginners.
**Three-piece plastic airlocks** — Traditional brewing airlocks fitted into jar lids via grommets. Filled with water to create a one-way valve. Inexpensive and reliable.

**Water-seal crocks** — The gutter around the crock rim is filled with water, and the lid sits in this gutter. CO₂ bubbles out through the water while oxygen cannot enter. The gold standard for large-batch fermentation.
**Loose-fitting lids** — Simply resting a lid on the jar (without tightening) allows gas exchange. Less effective than true airlocks but better than sealed lids.

sections.content: Keeping vegetables submerged below the brine line is the single most important step for preventing mold and ensuring even fermentation. Fermentation weights (also called followers) sit on top of the vegetables and push them down into the brine [cite:c2].

items: **Glass fermentation weights** — Purpose-made glass discs sized to fit inside mason jars or crocks. Heavy, non-reactive, easy to clean. The most popular option.
**Ceramic weights** — Often included with fermentation crocks. Semi-circular stones that nest together inside the crock.

**Zip-lock bag of brine** — A food-safe zip-lock bag filled with 2–3% salt brine, placed on top of the vegetables. Conforms to the jar shape and creates a seal. Use brine (not plain water) so that if the bag leaks, it doesn't dilute your ferment.
**Small jar or ramekin** — A smaller jar filled with water and placed inside the mouth of the fermentation jar. Simple and effective.
**Cabbage leaf** — A large outer cabbage leaf pressed over the surface of the vegetables. Traditional and free.

sections.content: Accurate measurement is the difference between consistent results and guesswork. Two measuring tools stand out as particularly valuable for fermenters:

items: **Kitchen scale** — Essential for measuring salt by weight, which is far more accurate than measuring by volume. A basic digital scale accurate to 1 gram is sufficient and costs under $15. Weigh your vegetables, calculate the salt percentage, and weigh the salt [[article:salt-and-fermentation]].

**pH strips or pH meter** — Useful for confirming that your ferment has reached a safe pH (below 4.6). pH strips (about $5–10 for 100 strips) are adequate for most home use. A digital pH meter ($15–50) offers more precision but requires calibration and electrode care [cite:c3].
**Thermometer** — Helpful for monitoring fermentation temperature, especially in warm climates or during summer months. Fermentation is faster at higher temperatures and slower at lower ones.
**Measuring spoons and cups** — For brine recipes that specify volume measurements. Less accurate than weight-based measurement but acceptable for simple brines.

sections.content: Once you've been fermenting for a while and want to expand your capabilities, these tools open up new possibilities:

items: **Mandoline or food processor** — For consistent, thin slicing of cabbage and other vegetables. Uniform cuts ferment more evenly.
**Vegetable tamper / pounder** — A wooden or plastic tool for packing vegetables tightly into jars and extracting brine from salted vegetables.
**Fermentation crock with water seal** — For serious, large-batch fermentation. Available in sizes from 5 to 30+ litres.
**Temperature-controlled chamber** — A mini-fridge with a temperature controller allows you to ferment at precise temperatures year-round. Particularly useful for long, slow ferments.
**Vacuum sealer** — Useful for packaging finished ferments for long-term storage or gifting.

sections.title: Best Value Upgrades
sections.content: If you're going to spend money on just two upgrades, make them a digital kitchen scale (for accurate salt measurement) and glass fermentation weights (for reliable submersion). These two items — totaling about $20–25 — will improve your results more than any other equipment investment.

citations.text: Katz, Sandor Ellix. The Art of Fermentation: An In-Depth Exploration of Essential Concepts and Processes from Around the World.
citations.author: Sandor Ellix Katz

citations.text: Shockey, Kirsten K., and Shockey, Christopher. Fermented Vegetables: Creative Recipes for Fermenting 64 Vegetables & Herbs.
citations.author: Kirsten K. Shockey and Christopher Shockey
